text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING STUDY — METASTATIC PANCREATIC ADENOCARCINOMA, CYCLE 3 COMPLETION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old woman with a known history of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head/uncinate process, initially borderline resectable, treated with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) followed by concurrent cape-based chemoradiation, and subsequently resected via pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with negative surgical margins (R0). Final pathologic stage was pT3 pN1 M0 (Stage IIB). She completed adjuvant Gemzar plus cape (6 cycles), complicated by a grade B pancreatic fistula requiring hospitalization and dose reductions. Surveillance imaging approximately 12 months after completing adjuvant chemotherapy demonstrated a new 1.2 cm hepatic lesion in segment VI and two small pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 7 mm, left lower lobe 5 mm), accompanied by rising CA 19-9 (67 U/mL) and CEA (4.8 ng/mL). Biopsy of the hepatic lesion confirmed metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. She has been receiving palliative-intent gem (1,000 mg/m2) plus Abraxane (100 mg/m2, dose-reduced) on a 28-day cycle schedule. This study is obtained following completion of cycle 3 for restaging and treatment response assessment.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350). Acquisitions were obtained in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis and in the pulmonary arterial phase for the chest. Axial images were reconstructed at 2.5 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reformats generated in coronal and sagittal planes. Comparison is made to the prior restaging CT obtained approximately 3 months earlier, which first identified the hepatic and pulmonary findings.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally without lobar or segmental consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion. Two previously described pulmonary nodules are again identified and are essentially unchanged in size and character. The right lower lobe nodule in the posterior basal segment measures approximately 7 mm in greatest diameter (unchanged from 7 mm on prior CT). The left lower lobe nodule in the posteromedial basal segment measures approximately 5 mm (unchanged from 5 mm on prior CT). Both nodules are solid, smoothly marginated, and subpleural in location. No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No spiculated, irregularly marginated, or cavitary lesions. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleura: No pleural effusions. No pleural thickening or pleural-based nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is unremarkable in caliber. Mediastinal contours are within normal limits. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The thymus is not enlarged. No anterior mediastinal masses.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures: No lytic or blastic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. No rib lesions.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with a smooth contour. Post-surgical anatomy is noted consistent with the known prior hepaticojejunostomy, though the hepatic parenchyma itself is unremarkable. The previously identified hypodense lesion in hepatic segment VI is again demonstrated. On this portal venous phase acquisition, the lesion appears as a hypoattenuating focus measuring approximately 1.2 cm in greatest axial dimension, essentially stable when compared with the prior examination (1.2 cm previously). The morphology and enhancement characteristics are consistent with the previously biopsied metastatic deposit. There is no appreciable change in lesion size or internal architecture. No new hepatic lesions are identified. No hepatic cysts or hemangiomas of concern. No biliary ductal dilatation. The common bile duct, as reconstructed at the hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis, appears patent without upstream dilatation.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation without focal lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreatic remnant (body and tail) is markedly attenuated in caliber as expected following pancreaticoduodenectomy. There is a small amount of peripancreatic and peri-anastomotic fibrotic tissue at the pancreaticojejunostomy site, unchanged from the prior study. No new focal mass lesions within the pancreatic remnant. No pancreatic ductal dilatation within the remnant. No peripancreatic fluid collections, pseudocysts, or abscess formation.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands: The kidneys are symmetric in size and enhance uniformly without focal cortical lesions. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding. The adrenal glands are normal in morphology bilaterally without nodular thickening or mass.

Gastrointestinal Tract: Post-Whipple anatomy is again noted. The gastrojejunostomy anastomosis is intact without evidence of obstruction or leak. No bowel wall thickening or obstruction identified. The colon is unremarkable throughout its visible extent. No appendiceal pathology.

Retroperitoneum and Peritoneum: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes. Nodes in the porta hepatis, peripancreatic, and para-aortic stations do not meet size criteria for pathologic enlargement and are stable or mildly decreased compared to prior. No peritoneal nodularity or soft tissue implants suggestive of peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.

Vascular Structures: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilatation. The superior mesenteric artery and celiac axis are patent and unremarkable. The portal vein and its tributaries are patent without thrombosis.

PELVIS:

Pelvic Organs: The uterus and adnexa appear unremarkable for age. No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic floor masses or implants.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and smooth-walled without intraluminal filling defects.

Osseous Structures (Abdomen and Pelvis): No suspicious lytic or sclerotic bone lesions. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine and sacroiliac joints consistent with the patient's age.

Soft Tissues: No subcutaneous or muscular soft tissue masses.

IMPRESSION:

1. The single known hepatic metastasis in segment VI is stable in size, measuring approximately 1.2 cm, without appreciable interval change compared to the prior examination. No new hepatic lesions are identified.

2. The two previously described pulmonary nodules — right lower lobe (7 mm) and left lower lobe (5 mm) — are stable in size and morphology. No new pulmonary nodules.

3. No new sites of metastatic disease are identified in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No peritoneal implants. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes.

4. Post-surgical changes from prior pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy are stable. No peripancreatic fluid collections, anastomotic complications, or local tumor recurrence identified.

5. Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with stable metastatic disease following three cycles of palliative gem and Abraxane. In the appropriate clinical context and with reference to serum tumor markers (CA 19-9 reportedly declining), these findings support a favorable early response to ongoing systemic therapy.

Signed electronically by: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging Section, Department of Radiology
